message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To evaluate whether the Families First Coronavirus Response Act (FFCRA) modified the association between pre-existing state paid sick leave (PSL) and weekday workplace mobility between February 15 and July 7, 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The 50 US states and Washington, D.C. were divided into exposure groups based on the presence or absence of pre-existing state PSL policies. Derived from Google COVID-19 Community Mobility Reports, the outcome was measured as the daily percent change in weekday workplace mobility. Mixed-effects, interrupted time series regression was performed to evaluate weekday workplace mobility after the implementation of the FFCRA on April 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    , 2020.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    States with pre-existing PSL policies exhibited a greater drop in mobility following the passage of the FFCRA (
                    <jats:bold>β</jats:bold>
                    =-8.86,95%CI:-11.6,-6.10,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    < 001). This remained significant after adjusting for state-level health, economic, and sociodemographic indicators (
                    <jats:bold>β</jats:bold>
                    =-3.13,95%CI:-5.92,-0.34,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =.039).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Pre-existing PSL policies contributed to a significant decline in weekday workplace mobility after the FFCRA, which may have influenced local health outcomes.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Policy implications</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The presence of pre-existing state policies may differentially influence the impact of federal legislation enacted during emergencies.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: The Impact of State Paid Sick Leave Policies on Longitudinal Weekday Workplace Mobility During the COVID-19 Pandemic
